Fern PoLrrrcAL PnecrrcEs ConaMrssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886 Corrected Version: JuIy 29,2003
July 24,2003
Sue Horne
Nevada County
Board of Supervisors
950 Maidu Avenue
Nevada City, CA 95959-8617
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-03-095
Dear Ms. Home:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').1
QUESTION
May you participate in a decision which may result in the altering of requirements
of the Second Unit Pilot Program?
CONCLUSION
You may participate in such a decision because, based on your facts, it is not
reasonably foreseeable that this decision will have a material financial effect on your
economic interests.
FACTS
You are the 2003 chairman of the Nevada County Board of Supervisors,
representing Diskict II. The circumstances of your (as secretary-director) and your
husband's general contracting business remains the same as in Horne Advice Letter No.
A-00-268, your previous request for advice. Specifically, your husband is a general
building contractor and primarily contracts with individual clients to build their custom
homes. You are the secretary-director of the corporation. He builds tlrpically two to
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.


File No. 4-03-095
Page No. 2
three new custom homes per year. The remainder of his business is remodeling existing
homes, againtypically two to three per year, generally in Nevada County but also in
neighboring Placer County. Your husband has had 3 new construction customers and 3
remodeling customers in the past 12 months. None of these clients, who are individuals,
are involved with home construction as a business.
Your corporation is a current member of the Nevada County Contractors'
Association. You estimate the number of conskuction permits for single-family
residences issued per yeff by the County of Nevada remains around 400 and there are
still approximately 350 licensed general building contractors in Nevada County with 200
in western Nevada County.
The Nevada County Board of Supervisors has a three-year pilot-housing program
in effect, which permits a second dwelling unit (maximum 1,000 square feet) to be
constructed on a parcel of land by a property owner when specific requirements are met.
This pilot program allows for 30 second housing units to be built ayear, with apossible
pilot program within the first year. Due to concerns in the communityregarding the
alleged overly restrictive requirements of the program, which discourage theutilization of
the program, you requested, and the board of supervisors voted to form a Second Unit
Pilot Program Task Force for the board's consideration and possible action.
There is no supervisor sitting on the task force which has completed its work and
is prepared to submit its report with recommendations to the board.
The potential project applications for the proposed 90-second unit construction
projects are unknown at this time. Your husband's business has no business or
professional relationships for the purpose ofbuilding a second unit nor does he have any
intention or plan to build a second unit if the board of supervisors alters the existing
provisions of the Second Unit Pilot Program.
ANALYSIS
The primary purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests ofpersons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of
this goal, section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section 87100 requires
analysis of the following questions as outlined below.2
2 These questions are based on ths Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at regulation I S700(b)


File No. 4-03-095
Page No. 3
Steps One and Two: Are you considered a s6public official" making, participating in
making or influencing a governmental decision?
As a member of the Nevada County Board of Supervisors, you are a "member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agenct''and, therefore, a
public official subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048;
regulation 18701(a).)
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation 18702.1.)
You will "make a govemmental decision" if you vote on a decision which will
result in the altering of requirements of the Second Unit Pilot Program. Additionally, if
you engage in any of the actions described in enclosed regulations 18702.2 and 18702.3
with regard to such a decision, that will constitute "participating in making" or
"infl uencing" that decision.
Step Three: What are your economic interests - 
the possible source of a conflict of
interest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
offioial, a member of his or her immediate family,l',or on any of the officialls economic. .
interests, described as follows:
a
a
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment 3 of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a);
regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (section S7103(d); regulation
1 8703.1(b));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2);
3 An indirect investnent or interest means any investrnent or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the offrcial's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public ofEcial, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) o'Immediate family" is defined
at section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.


a
File No. 4-03-095
Page No. 4
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03(c); regulation I 8703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 I 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87103; regulation 18703.5).
a
a
General Contracting Business
Provided you or your husbanda has invested $2,000 or more in his general
contracting business, you have an economic interest in this business entity.
In addition, the Act defines "income", in relevant part, as:
"(a) 'Income' means, except as provided in subdivision
(b), a payment received, including but not limited to any
salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds
from any sale, gift, including any glft of food orbeverage,
loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by
the filer, reimbursernent for expenses, per diem, or
contribution to an insurance orpension program paid by
anyperson other than an,employer, and including any
communityproperty interest in the income of a spouse.
Income also includes an outstanding loan. Income of an
individual also includes a pro rata share of any income of
anybusiness entity or trust in which the individual or
spouse owns, directly, indirectly or benefici ally, a
l0-percent interest or greater. olncome,' other than a gtft,
does not include income received from any source outside
the jurisdiction and not doing business within the
jurisdiction, not planning to do business within the
jurisdiction, or not having done business within the
jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time any
statement or other action is required under this title.,,
(Section 82030.)
Therefore, you also have an economic interest in this business as a source of
income to you if your communityproperty interest in income received from this business
was $500 or more of income within 12 months preceding the time that the relevant
decision is made.
a See Footnote 3 addressing "indirect" interest.


File No. A-03-095
Page No. 5
You also have an economic interest in this business based on your position as
secretary-director. (Section 87103(d).)
Clients of General Contractine Business
Finally, since your husband is the owner of the general contracting business, the
clients of this business are considered sources of income to you if your community
property interest in your spouse's income from a particular client is $500 or more.s
(Section 82030; see also Dillon Advice Letter,No. I-02-082.)
You have not provided information regardin g arry other economic interests. For
purposes of this letter, we assume you have no other economic interests relevant to the
decision you have identified.6
Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?
A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of the
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
5 You have not identified specific clients in which you have such an economic interest. However,
you have stated that your husband has had 3 new conskuction customers and 3 remodeling customers in the
past 12 months and that none of these clients, who are individuals, are involved with home construction as
a business. As such, the information you have provided does not indicate that a conflict of interest in the
second unit decision will arise from yow economic interests in one of these clients. Therefore, we do not
analyze these economic interests further. Nevertheless, if you, in applying the Commission's rules, are
aware that the decision may have a material financial effect on one of these clients, you should seek further
advice as we only analyze herein whether you have a coaflict of interest with respect to your husband's
general contracting business.
6 Please note regulation 18705.5(a) currently provides: "When determining whether a
govenrmental decision has a material effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her personal
finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real properly owned directly or indirectly by the official,
nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value ofassets and liabilities ofa business entity
in which the official has a direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered." Consequently, we do
not atalyze your economic interest in your personal finances (as we did in our previous letter to you) for
purposes of this letter. Rather, we consider the effect of the decision on your economic interest in your
spouse's business.


File No. 4-03-095
Page No. 6
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.,,
(Regulation I 870a. I (a).)
Because your spouse's business has not initiated the proceeding in which
decisions regarding the altering of the requirements for the Second Unit Pilot program
will be considered, his business is indirectly involved for purposes of the Act.
Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
Regulation 18705.1(c) applies to sources of income which are business entities
indirectly involved in a decision. (Regulation 18705.3(bxl).)
Regulation 18705.1(c) provides the materiality standards applicable to business
entities indirectly involved in a decision. Subdivision (c)(1) of regulation 18705.1
applies only where a business entity is listed on the Fortune 500. Subdivisions (c)e) -
(3) of regulation 18705.1 apply where a business entity is listed on a certain exchange
(e.9, the New York Stock Exchange, the NASDAQ, or the American Stock Exchange);
in addition, subdivisions (c)(2) -(cX3) apply where a business entity is not listed o, Lr"
of the specified exchanges but altemate earnings/income criteria for the entity are met.
Finally, subdivision (cX4) applies to business entities, such as small businesses, not
covered by subdivisions (c)(l )-(3).
We have previously applied the materiality standard applicable to a small
business to your spouse's business. Since the Horne Advice Letter, No. 4-00-268, was
issued to you, the monetary thresholds for small businesses also have been doubled.T
Pursuant to this materiality standard, the financial effect of a government decision on the
business entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that :
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.1(c)(a).)
' The Commission amended this regulation with doubled monetary thresholds so that it became effective
February 1,2001.


File No. 4-03-095
Page No. 7
Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon any of your economic interests will meet the applicable materiality
standard?
After identifying the applicable materiality standards, you must then assess
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect your husband,s business
entity or your sources of income in an amount that rises to the monetary level considered
mateial for that particular economic interest. If it is reasonably foreseiable that the
monetary threshold will be met as a result of the decision, then you will have a conflict of
interest arising from that business entity or source of income and will be prohibited from
participating in the decision.
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable,, if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 1S706(a).) A financial ef,fect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops. 198.)
You must assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision in question
will materially affect the general contracting business's expenses, assets, Iiabilities, or
gross revenues by the monetary amounts specified in regulation 18705.1(c)(4). (See
above.)
The question of whether financial consequences on a business entity are
reasonably foreseeable atthe time of a govemmental decision is made must always
depend on the facts of each particular case. (In re Thorner, supra.) Thus, we cannot
categorically state at this time whether the Second Unit Pilot Program decision you have
identified will or will not have areasonably foreseeable material financial effectupon
your spouse's business. Nevertheless, the Thorner opinion discusses facts similarlo
those you present which are instructive in the present circumstances.
In the Thorneropinion, the Commission compared a situation where a contractor
who was a regular customer of the pubtic official's hardware business was preparing to
bid on, or had bid on, a project that was the subject of a decision before the officiallo a
scenario where the contractor was already awarded the contract, but had not yet
purchased or agreed to purchase any of the official's materials for the projeci. ffre
Commission stated:
"There is a significant difference between the two situations....[A]lthough
there is no certainty that McPhail's will receive business, there is u rrigtr-
probability that it will since the contractor who has been awarded the
contract is a regular customer. Although there is no agreement, express or
implied fcitation omitted] between Mcphail's and the contractor, there is,
without question, a sufficient likelihood that Mcphail's will receive
business to make the financial effect on Director Mcphail ,reasonably
foreseeable'."


File No. 4-03-095
Page No. 8
"...[In the other situation,] an extra degree of remoteness is added to the
foreseeability of the financial effect by reason of the fact that the
contractor has not yet been awarded the contract, but merely has entered a
bid or is preparing to do so. Mcphail's will benefit only if ihe contractor,s
bid is successful and the contractor follows its normal practice of
purchasing from McPhail's."
This "extra degree of remoteness," the Commission concluded, meant that the financial
effect was not reasonably foreseeable. (Thiltgen Advice Letter,No. I-03-053.)
In your sifuation, the general contracting business will not be affected unless
several events occur: First, the program's requirements must be altered. Second, these
changes must be such that they could create a financial effect on your spouse,s business.
Third, your spouse's business must engage in business activities that would cause any
requirernent changes to actually affectthe business. Finallr the financial effect must
amount to one of the monetary thresholds of regulation 1g705.1(c)(4).
The facts you provide exhibit the "extra degree of remoteness" which, in the
Thorner opinion, indicates that decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial
efflect upon an official's economic interest. (See OlsonAdvice Leiter,No. 4-00-237.) In
particular, you stated that there are as many as 350 contractors within Nevada County
who could presumably bid on any contract to build second units and that your husband
has no plans to build any such units. Thus, unless you are aware that changes in the
program's requirements will create a financial effect on your spouse's business or on
another economic interest of yours which meets an applicable materiality standard, the
element of reasonable foreseeability necessary for the existence of a conflict of interest
will be lacking.
Nevertheless, because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in
providing advice (In re Ogksby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), the determination of whether it
is reasonably foreseeable that an applicable materiality standard will be met for any of
your economic interests is necessat'rly afacfual question that is ultimately for you to
decide.
Steps Seven and Eight: The 66Public Generally" and "LegaIIy Required, Exceptions
The facts you have presented do not suggest that the "public generally:? exception
or the "legally required participation" rule, exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules, is
applicable to your situation.


File No. 4-03-095
Page No. 9
If you have any other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at (et6)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
(.e vVY.<
Natalie
Enclosures
NBje
I:\Adviceltrs\03-095
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
May you participate in a decision which may result in the altering of requirements
of the Second Unit Pilot Program?
sections.conclusion:
You may participate in such a decision because, based on your facts, it is not
reasonably foreseeable that this decision will have a material financial effect on your
economic interests.

loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by

anyperson other than an,employer, and including any

A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either

made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar
request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the
proceeding concerning the decision before the official or
the official's agency. A person is the subject of the
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
5 You have not identified specific clients in which you have such an economic interest. However,

other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.,,
(Regulation I 870a. I (a).)
Because your spouse's business has not initiated the proceeding in which
decisions regarding the altering of the requirements for the Second Unit Pilot program
will be considered, his business is indirectly involved for purposes of the Act.
Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
Regulation 18705.1(c) applies to sources of income which are business entities
indirectly involved in a decision. (Regulation 18705.3(bxl).)
Regulation 18705.1(c) provides the materiality standards applicable to business
entities indirectly involved in a decision. Subdivision (c)(1) of regulation 18705.1
applies only where a business entity is listed on the Fortune 500. Subdivisions (c)e) -
(3) of regulation 18705.1 apply where a business entity is listed on a certain exchange
(e.9, the New York Stock Exchange, the NASDAQ, or the American Stock Exchange);
in addition, subdivisions (c)(2) -(cX3) apply where a business entity is not listed o, Lr"
of the specified exchanges but altemate earnings/income criteria for the entity are met.
Finally, subdivision (cX4) applies to business entities, such as small businesses, not
covered by subdivisions (c)(l )-(3).
We have previously applied the materiality standard applicable to a small
business to your spouse's business. Since the Horne Advice Letter, No. 4-00-268, was
issued to you, the monetary thresholds for small businesses also have been doubled.T
Pursuant to this materiality standard, the financial effect of a government decision on the
business entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that :
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.1(c)(a).)
' The Commission amended this regulation with doubled monetary thresholds so that it became effective
February 1,2001.
Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon any of your economic interests will meet the applicable materiality
standard?
After identifying the applicable materiality standards, you must then assess
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect your husband,s business
entity or your sources of income in an amount that rises to the monetary level considered
mateial for that particular economic interest. If it is reasonably foreseiable that the
monetary threshold will be met as a result of the decision, then you will have a conflict of
interest arising from that business entity or source of income and will be prohibited from
participating in the decision.
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable,, if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 1S706(a).) A financial ef,fect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops. 198.)
You must assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision in question
will materially affect the general contracting business's expenses, assets, Iiabilities, or
gross revenues by the monetary amounts specified in regulation 18705.1(c)(4). (See
above.)
The question of whether financial consequences on a business entity are
reasonably foreseeable atthe time of a govemmental decision is made must always
depend on the facts of each particular case. (In re Thorner, supra.) Thus, we cannot
categorically state at this time whether the Second Unit Pilot Program decision you have
identified will or will not have areasonably foreseeable material financial effectupon
your spouse's business. Nevertheless, the Thorner opinion discusses facts similarlo
those you present which are instructive in the present circumstances.
In the Thorneropinion, the Commission compared a situation where a contractor
who was a regular customer of the pubtic official's hardware business was preparing to
bid on, or had bid on, a project that was the subject of a decision before the officiallo a
scenario where the contractor was already awarded the contract, but had not yet
purchased or agreed to purchase any of the official's materials for the projeci. ffre
Commission stated:
"There is a significant difference between the two situations....[A]lthough
there is no certainty that McPhail's will receive business, there is u rrigtr-
probability that it will since the contractor who has been awarded the
contract is a regular customer. Although there is no agreement, express or
implied fcitation omitted] between Mcphail's and the contractor, there is,
without question, a sufficient likelihood that Mcphail's will receive
business to make the financial effect on Director Mcphail ,reasonably
foreseeable'."
"...[In the other situation,] an extra degree of remoteness is added to the
foreseeability of the financial effect by reason of the fact that the
contractor has not yet been awarded the contract, but merely has entered a
bid or is preparing to do so. Mcphail's will benefit only if ihe contractor,s
bid is successful and the contractor follows its normal practice of
purchasing from McPhail's."
This "extra degree of remoteness," the Commission concluded, meant that the financial
effect was not reasonably foreseeable. (Thiltgen Advice Letter,No. I-03-053.)
In your sifuation, the general contracting business will not be affected unless
several events occur: First, the program's requirements must be altered. Second, these
changes must be such that they could create a financial effect on your spouse,s business.
Third, your spouse's business must engage in business activities that would cause any
requirernent changes to actually affectthe business. Finallr the financial effect must
amount to one of the monetary thresholds of regulation 1g705.1(c)(4).
The facts you provide exhibit the "extra degree of remoteness" which, in the
Thorner opinion, indicates that decisions will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial
efflect upon an official's economic interest. (See OlsonAdvice Leiter,No. 4-00-237.) In
particular, you stated that there are as many as 350 contractors within Nevada County
who could presumably bid on any contract to build second units and that your husband
has no plans to build any such units. Thus, unless you are aware that changes in the
program's requirements will create a financial effect on your spouse's business or on
another economic interest of yours which meets an applicable materiality standard, the
element of reasonable foreseeability necessary for the existence of a conflict of interest
will be lacking.
Nevertheless, because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in
providing advice (In re Ogksby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), the determination of whether it
is reasonably foreseeable that an applicable materiality standard will be met for any of
your economic interests is necessat'rly afacfual question that is ultimately for you to
decide.
Steps Seven and Eight: The 66Public Generally" and "LegaIIy Required, Exceptions
The facts you have presented do not suggest that the "public generally:? exception
or the "legally required participation" rule, exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules, is
applicable to your situation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May you participate in a decision which may result in the altering of requirements
of the Second Unit Pilot Program?

CONCLUSION: You may participate in such a decision because, based on your facts, it is not
reasonably foreseeable that this decision will have a material financial effect on your
economic interests.
embedding.first_500_words: Fern PoLrrrcAL PnecrrcEs ConaMrssroN 428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886 Corrected Version: JuIy 29,2003 July 24,2003 Sue Horne Nevada County Board of Supervisors 950 Maidu Avenue Nevada City, CA 95959-8617 Re Your Request for Advice Our FiIe No. A-03-095 Dear Ms. Home: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').1 QUESTION May you participate in a decision which may result in the altering of requirements of the Second Unit Pilot Program? CONCLUSION You may participate in such a decision because, based on your facts, it is not reasonably foreseeable that this decision will have a material financial effect on your economic interests. FACTS You are the 2003 chairman of the Nevada County Board of Supervisors, representing Diskict II. The circumstances of your (as secretary-director) and your husband's general contracting business remains the same as in Horne Advice Letter No. A-00-268, your previous request for advice. Specifically, your husband is a general building contractor and primarily contracts with individual clients to build their custom homes. You are the secretary-director of the corporation. He builds tlrpically two to I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations. File No. 4-03-095 Page No. 2 three new custom homes per year. The remainder of his business is remodeling existing homes, againtypically two to three per year, generally in Nevada County but also in neighboring Placer County. Your husband has had 3 new construction customers and 3 remodeling customers in the past 12 months. None of these clients, who are individuals, are involved with home construction as a business. Your corporation is a current member of the Nevada County Contractors' Association. You estimate the number of conskuction permits for single-family residences issued per yeff by the County of Nevada remains around 400 and there are still approximately 350 licensed general building contractors in Nevada County with 200 in western Nevada County. The Nevada County Board of Supervisors has a three-year pilot-housing program in effect, which permits a second dwelling unit (maximum 1,000 square feet) to be constructed on a parcel of land by a property owner when specific requirements are met. This pilot program allows for 30 second housing units to be built ayear, with apossible pilot program within the first year. Due to concerns in the communityregarding the alleged overly restrictive requirements of the program, which discourage theutilization of the program, you requested, and the board of supervisors voted to form a Second Unit Pilot Program Task Force for the board's consideration and possible action. There is no supervisor sitting on the task force which has completed its work and is prepared to submit its report with recommendations to the board. The potential project applications for the proposed 90-second unit construction projects are unknown at this time. Your husband's business has no business or professional relationships for the purpose ofbuilding
